coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric detection of CIP and bioimaging employing a fluorescent nanoprobe with blue emission using carbon quantum dots from papaya seeds (Carica papaya)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Govindhan, Moganesh
Selvaraj, Sureshkumar
Sukumar, Kalpana
coredata.dc:description: A growing burden of industrial and ecological biowaste requires techniques to convert the biowaste into the active moiety of interest. Among the numerous materials investigated, we emphasized the utilization of a possible carbon-containing precursor in papaya seeds (PS) that transforms into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) without the use of chemical agents. The development of sensitive and selective ciprofloxacin (CIP) detection via the microwave-assisted method of preparing blue fluorescent CQDs from papaya seeds is unique to our research. We examined the morphology, crystal structure, functional groups and photoluminescence properties in this work. The fluorescence intensity diminished while the CIP concentration was increased from 0 to 150 µM, and the detection limit was 0.37 µM, indicating that the prepared PS-CQDs may be employed as a CIP sensing agent. The N2 worms treated with PS-CQDs showed blue color emission mainly in the pharyngeal region and mid-body of the nematodes. This study reveals a significant glimpse into the benefits of turning bio-waste into carbon quantum dots for use in fluorescence sensing and bio-imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green extraction of CQDS
Papaya seeds
Microwave-assisted method
Detection of CIP
Bio imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PS-CQDSs by the following procedure
Optical sensing of pharmaceutical pollutant ciprofloxacin by the prepared PS-CQDs
Bio-imaging application of PS-CQDs in the in-vivo model C. elegans

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of prepared PS-CQDs and their applications in CIP sensing and bio imaging.
XRD pattern of the Physicochemical characteristics of prepared PS-CQDs.
Physicochemical characteristics of prepared PS-CQDs (a) Raman and (b) FTIR.
Physicochemical characteristics of prepared PS-CQDs. (a) PL emission, and (b) UV-Vis absorption spectra.
Physicochemical characteristics of prepared PS-CQDs. (a) HR TEM, (b) SEAD, (c) Particle size distribution and (d) Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX).

a) pH effect on PL. b) Temperature effect on PL. c) Detection of Ciprofloxacin at concentrations ranging from 0 to 150 µM. d) PS-CQDS stability in the presence of a variety of interferents. e) A calibration plot for PS-CQDSs demonstrating a linear relationship of 0.997.
Bioimaging application of PS-CQDs in the in-vivo model C. elegans (N2 worms) after treatment with PS-CQDs for 30 min. The arrowhead indicates the secondary targeted organ of the nematodes.
Comparison of the performance and efficiency of the proposed method of CQD with the existing detection of the CIP sensor.
Recovery of CIP towards milk powder (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorometric detection of CIP and bioimaging employing a fluorescent nanoprobe with blue emission using carbon quantum dots from papaya seeds (Carica papaya)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Saveetha Engineering College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A growing burden of industrial and ecological biowaste requires techniques to convert the biowaste into the active moiety of interest. Among the numerous materials investigated, we emphasized the utilization of a possible carbon-containing precursor in papaya seeds (PS) that transforms into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) without the use of chemical agents. The development of sensitive and selective ciprofloxacin (CIP) detection via the microwave-assisted method of preparing blue fluorescent CQDs from papaya seeds is unique to our research. We examined the morphology, crystal structure, functional groups and photoluminescence properties in this work. The fluorescence intensity diminished while the CIP concentration was increased from 0 to 150 µM, and the detection limit was 0.37 µM, indicating that the prepared PS-CQDs may be employed as a CIP sensing agent. The N2 worms treated with PS-CQDs showed blue color emission mainly in the pharyngeal region and mid-body of the nematodes. This study reveals a significant glimpse into the benefits of turning bio-waste into carbon quantum dots for use in fluorescence sensing and bio-imaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Faced with the ever-increasing burden of polyfluous anthropogenic and ecological biowaste, urgent answers are desperately needed for viable waste management and environmental protection in the future. One potential answer to addressing the problem is the conversion of biowaste to useful nanostructures or nanomaterials, like carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have unique optical and chemical properties. [1–3] Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has garnered significant attention due to its eco-friendly approach, utilizing natural and renewable resources. This method aligns with sustainable practices by avoiding toxic chemicals and high-energy processes.

Numerous biowaste sources have been explored for CQD synthesis. For example, lemon peel waste has been used to produce water-soluble CQDs via hydrothermal treatment. These CQDs exhibited excellent photoluminescence and were effective in detecting Cr⁶⁺ ions and enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO₂ nanofibers. [4] Similarly, neem (Azadirachta indica) leaves have yielded fluorescent CQDs with high quantum yield and peroxidase-like activity, enabling the detection of hydrogen peroxide and ascorbic acid in fruit samples. [5] CQDs derived from Tamarindus indica leaves demonstrated a quantum yield of approximately 46.6 % and were utilized to detect mercury(II) ions and glutathione.[6] Bark from Ficus religiosa served as a precursor for CQDs capable of selectively detecting Fe³ ⁺ ions in water samples, highlighting their utility in environmental monitoring. [7] CQDs synthesized from red cabbage showed strong antioxidant activity and biocompatibility, making them suitable for biomedical applications.[8] CQDs derived from papaya (Carica papaya) seeds have also shown potential, exhibiting blue fluorescence useful for cellular imaging. Their small size and surface functional groups facilitate efficient cellular uptake with minimal cytotoxicity, essential traits for bioimaging applications. Beyond imaging, CQDs have been explored for detecting antibiotics like ciprofloxacin (CIP) through fluorescence-based sensing. The fluorescence of CQDs can be selectively quenched or enhanced in the presence of specific analytes, enabling the development of sensitive, selective, and low-cost detection methods.[9,10]

This study investigates an eco-friendly approach for synthesizing fluorescent CQDs from papaya seed biowaste, a commonly discarded agricultural byproduct. These papaya seed-derived CQDs (PS-CQDs) were prepared via a microwave-assisted method without the use of chemical agents, offering a greener alternative to conventional synthesis techniques.[11,12] Ciprofloxacin is a widely used broad-spectrum antibiotic belonging to the fluoroquinolone class, commonly employed in both clinical and agricultural settings. However, its extensive use has led to environmental contamination, as CIP residues can leach into water supplies, posing health risks such as hepatotoxicity, nausea, headaches, and neurological complications.[13,14] Therefore, developing sensitive and cost-effective methods to detect trace levels of CIP in environmental and pharmaceutical samples is essential.

Conventional detection techniques such as liquid chromatography (LC), mass spectrometry (MS), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) are accurate but often require expensive instrumentation, complex sample preparation, and lack portability, limiting their use in real-time, on-site analysis. Consequently, CQD-based sensors derived from natural precursors are receiving growing interest as practical alternatives. Studies have reported CQD synthesis from various natural sources, including apple juice, lemon juice, sugarcane bagasse, and bamboo leaves, all utilizing green synthesis methods to minimize environmental impact.[15–17] CQDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials known for their high photoluminescence, tunable surface functionality, and biocompatibility. While various synthesis methods such as arc discharge, laser ablation, hydrothermal treatment, and chemical oxidation have been employed [18–26], many suffer from drawbacks including high energy demands, costly reagents, and long processing times. In contrast, microwave-assisted synthesis offers a rapid, cost-effective, and scalable route, particularly when using renewable carbon sources.[27,28]

In this work, we report for the first time the green synthesis of PS-CQDs using papaya seeds and a microwave-assisted technique. The resulting CQDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence, excellent water solubility, and low cytotoxicity. Their structural and optical properties were comprehensively characterized. Most notably, PS-CQDs were employed as fluorescent probes for ciprofloxacin detection via a fluorescence-quenching mechanism, achieving a detection limit of 0.37 µM. Additionally, bioimaging experiments using Caenorhabditis elegans (N2 strain) confirmed the biocompatibility and imaging capability of the PS-CQDs, with fluorescence observed in the pharyngeal and mid-body regions of the nematodes. These results highlight the dual functionality of PS-CQDs for environmental sensing and biological imaging and demonstrate a sustainable route for converting biowaste into valuable nanomaterials for future applications in nanobiotechnology.
The papayas were sourced from a home garden in Coimbatore, India, and Deionized water (DI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PS-CQDSs by the following procedure
ce:section.ce:para: In the present investigation, the PS-CQDs were synthesized from papaya seeds using a microwave-assisted method ( Fig. 1). The papaya seeds were shade dried and powdered using a mixer grinder. After that, 50 ml of distilled water was mixed with 2 mg of papaya seed and sonicated for 30 minutes. The centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 5 minutes. Then, the supernatant was collected and transferred into the microwave-assisted (CEM, Discover) and heated for 5 min to 200°C with the power of 100 W at a pressure of 100 psi. The solution was centrifuged at 12,500 rpm, and the supernatant was further purified using a membrane filter (0.22 μm) and dialysis membrane (3500 MWCO). The final product was lyophilized for further use. The quantum yield of the prepared PS-CQDSs was calculated according to the method described

The Photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (PLQY) was determined by comparing both absorbance and PL intensities of PS-CQDs and quinine sulfate. The PLQY equation is as follows:
(1)
Q
Y
S
= Q
Y
S T
I
S
I
S T
η
S
2
η
S T
2
A
S T
A
S

where “S” and “ST” denote the PS-CQDs and reference (quinine sulfate), “A” denotes the absorbance values of the PS-CQDs and reference, “I” denotes the PL intensities of the PS-CQDs and reference, and “η” denotes the refractive index of the PS-CQDs and reference, respectively (both are 1.33). As a standard for PS-CQDs, the reference (quinine sulfate) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 with a QYST of 54 %.

The crystalline phase and crystallite size of PS-CQDs were measured using an X-ray diffraction pattern generated at room temperature using a powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) device loaded with CuK radiation (1,5406). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy(HRTEM) was used to examine the PS-CQDs' morphology (JEOL JEM 2100). The functional group of PS-CQDs was identified using Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (BRUKER, TENSOR 27). The chemical composition of PS-CQDs was determined using Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Jobin Yvon Lab Ram-HR). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was determined using Fluoromax-4, a spectrofluorometer.

The CQD dispersion was used to test the selectivity of glycine, enrofloxacin, levofloxacin, cysteine, ciprofloxacin, and histidine. A 2 ml of different potential interferents with DI water were sonicated at room temperature for 5 minutes using 2 ml of PS-CQDs solution (0.04 mg/ml). Then the PL spectra were taken at 390 nm. It was then used to adjust the pH of the PS-CQDs solution (0.02 mg/ml, 2 ml) from acidic (3−5) to basic (9−11). The PL spectra were collected after 5 minutes of sonication.

The age-synchronized Bristol wild-type (N2) worms were grown in nematode growth medium as reported earlier [29]. The L4 stage worms were washed and transferred to 1 ml of M9 buffer and incubated with 100 µL of prepared PS-CQDS (1 mg/ml). After incubation for 30 min, the worms were washed thrice with M9 buffer, mounted on an agar-padded glass slide (1.5 %), and imaged under laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSM 710, Zeiss, Germany).

In fluorescence spectroscopy, quenching refers to the process by which the fluorescence intensity of a fluorophore (such as Carbon Quantum Dots) is decreased due to interaction with a quencher molecule (e.g., metal ions, biomolecules, etc.). F₀: Fluorescence intensity of the CQDs without the quencher and F: Fluorescence intensity of the CQDs with the quencher present.
The quenching effect is calculated as F/F₀
A lower F/F₀ value (closer to 0) indicates strong quenching, while a value close to 1 means weak or no quenching.
Calculating the quenching efficiency
Quenching Efficiency (%) = (1-F/F0) × 100

To evaluate the practical applicability of the proposed sensor, commercial milk powder was chosen as a real sample matrix due to its widespread consumption and potential for contamination with antibiotic residues. The sample was first dissolved in deionized water (1 g in 10 ml), followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 minutes to remove undissolved solids. The supernatant was filtered (0.22 µM membrane) to ensure clarity and then spiked with known concentrations of ciprofloxacin (49 µM, 98 µM, 147 µM).

All samples were analyzed in triplicate (n = 3) using the optimized PS-CQDs sensor under the following conditions:
•
pH: adjusted to 7.0 using phosphate buffer
•
Temperature: 25°C (room temperature)
•
Excitation wavelength: 390 nm
•
Emission measured at: 440 nm
•
Fluorescence intensity recorded after 5 min of incubation
The recovery percentage and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated to assess the accuracy and precision of the method.

The XRD diffraction pattern of PS-CQDs showed a broad peak centered at 20.67°, which indicates the carbon's highly disordered and amorphous nature ( Fig. 2). The interlayer spacing of PS-CQDs is 0.42 nm, and it is higher than that of graphite (0.34 nm). The increased interlayer distance might result from functional groups being incorporated into the carbon structure [30].

The Raman spectrum observed the microstructure of the PS-CQDs. It showed two distinct peaks around 1370 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to D and G bands ( Fig. 3a). The D-band denotes the structural defect, and the G-band denotes the vibration of E2 g phonon modes of sp2 carbon. The defect intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) is 0.85, showing fewer structural defects of PS-CQDs

FTIR spectroscopy was used to analyze the functional groups of prepared PS-CQDs (Fig. 3b). The absorption peaks at 3281, 1638, 1145, 1008, and 676 cm−1 are attributed to O-H, CC, C-OH, C-O, and C-H groups, respectively. UV–visible and PL spectra were analyzed to determine the optical characteristics of the PS-CQDs generated from papaya seeds. To study the fluorescent property of PS-CQDs, the sample was excited at different wavelength ranges ( Fig. 4a). The PS-CQDs showed excitation-independent fluorescent property with maximum emission (440 nm) at the excitation wavelength (390 nm). The quantum yield of prepared PS-CQDs is 30.02 % when compared with the standard quinine sulfate. The UV-absorption spectra of PS-CQDs show a prominent peak around 217 nm, and a minor peak at 338 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of a CC bond and the n-π* transition of a CO bond, respectively (Fig. 4b).

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy(HRTEM) determines the particle size distribution and surface morphology of the synthesized PS-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5(a). The PS-CQDs were monodispersed with a narrow size distribution and well separated from each other with a spherical morphology in aqueous media Fig. 5. The particle size distribution histogram of PS-CQDs was plotted from Image J software. The SEAD pattern of PS-CQDs showed the amorphous nature of the material(Fig. 5b). The average particle size of the PS-CQDs is found to be 3.5 nm in diameter shown in Fig. 5(c). The EDX spectra were collected using HREM to detect the various elements that were present in the PS-CQDs. The EDAX spectrum shows the presence of various elements such as C (mass- 56.75 %) and O (43.25 %) as shown in Fig. 5d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical sensing of pharmaceutical pollutant ciprofloxacin by the prepared PS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared PS-CQDs are further evaluated for the fluorescence detection of CIP under optimized pH and temperature. The pH optimization of PS-CQDs was carried out in varying pH (pH 1–11), and its fluorescence intensity was observed ( Fig. 6a). The pH-7 shows maximum fluorescence intensity when compared with other pH samples. Further, we have optimized the fluorescent intensity of PS-CQDs at varying temperatures (RT to 80°C). The fluorescent property of PS-CQDs increases with an increase in temperature and obtains maximum fluorescence intensity at 50°C, and after that, the fluorescence intensity decreases (Fig. 6b).

We performed the fluorescence quenching impact of CIP by using different concentrations of CIP (0–150 µM) to evaluate the sensitivity of PS-CQDs to CIP. The fluorescent intensity of PS-CQDs decreases with an increase in CIP concentrations (Fig. 6c). The “Turn-off ” fluorescence of PS-CQDs was observed with the addition of CIP. The fluorescence quenching of PS-CQDs in CIP solution may result in non-radiative electron transfer, in which partial electrons from PS-CQDs are transferred to surface states of CIP. Overall, the findings revealed that sensitive techniques with a broad linear range had been developed efficiently. Regarding their interaction between the PS-CQDs and ciprofloxacin, this is possible that hydrogen bonds between the PS-CQDs and ciprofloxacin were created on their surface of the PS-CQDs. Electrostatic interactions between the PS-CQDs and the fluoro-substituted aromatic ring of ciprofloxacin can occur, meaning that PS-CQDs and ciprofloxacin form complexes that result in fluorescence quenching.

Next performed the fluorescence sensing performance of PS-CQDs was performed at the optimized condition for the detection of CIP. The selectivity of the PS-CQDs is a critical parameter in sensing. We used different interferents like glycine, enrofloxacin, levofloxacin, cysteine, and histidine, and compared them with CIP. The CIP showed a high quenching effect (F/F0) on PS-CQDs when compared with other interferents (150 µM) at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm (Fig. 6d). The interaction between CIP and hydroxyl or carboxylic groups on the surface edge of PS-CQDs has been proposed to be a significant aspect of CIP detection.

The Stern–Volmer plot from the sensitivity analysis shows a perfect linear behavior, and it was determined that the regression coefficient R2 was 0.997. The fluorescence quenching of PS-CQDs showed good sensing performance of CIP from 0–150 µM with a lower detection limit of 0.37 µM (Fig. 6e). This study demonstrates that CIP may be identified at low concentrations and acts as an effective indicator of CIP. We proposed to evaluate the detection efficiency of our CIP sensor to that of other CQDs-based CIP sensors ( Table 1)

We recognized that only CIP produced a durable impact when compared with other interferents, indicating that our suggested PS-CQDs were effectively employed to detect CIP as the sensing platform. We compared the performance of the proposed method with some existing traditional detection methods using CQDs in terms of linear range, LOD, R2 value, and detection of materials. The PS-CQDs were employed to illustrate the real sample analysis for CIP detection. This was performed by purchasing a real sample of milk powder from the local market within Coimbatore. With the standard addition method, samples were prepared and spiked with CIP at concentrations were 49 µM (low), 98 µM (middle), and 147 µM (high) at a pH of 7. This method was used to study the recovery of real sample analysis. Under the same conditions, each sample was evaluated three times (n = 3). The recovery results, which vary from 98.026 % to 99.138 %, indicate that the proposed technique is more effective for CIP detection ( Table 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-imaging application of PS-CQDs in the in-vivo model C. elegans
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent property of the prepared PS-CQDs was evaluated for the in-vivo bio imaging application. The prepared PS-CQDs show the excitation-independent fluorescent property ( Fig. 7). The N2 worms treated with PS-CQDs showed blue color emission mainly in their pharyngeal region and mid-body of the nematodes. The low intensity of the PS-CQDs was observed in the tail region of nematodes. This could be due to the proper functioning of the intestinal barrier, which reduced the translocation of PS-CQDs in the secondary targeted organ of nematodes. This result shows that the prepared PS-CQDs can be a potential material for bio-imaging applications in the biomedical field.

Environmental impacts, including toxic chemical synthesis, exploitation of nonrenewable resources, and excessive energy consumption, have constantly challenged the industrial sector. “Sustainability” refers to the development of friendly technology and materials through attentive resource utilization. This implies creating low-cost, ecologically friendly protocols that are simple to configure, energy-efficient, and, ideally, recyclable. The goal of this study is to provide a method for converting papaya seeds into valuable carbon quantum dots. The blue-fluorescent CQDs have a high quantum yield of 30.02 % and an excitation wavelength of 390 nm with an emission of 440 nm. The fluorescence quenching effect of PS-CQDs in the presence of CIP (0–150 µM) enables CIP detection in an aqueous solution with a detection limit of 0.37 µM. The prepared PS-CQDs can be a potential material for bio-imaging applications in the biomedical field. Our findings can help drive the future development of safer nanomaterial formulations, allowing them to be used more often in environmental and medicinal applications.

(Applicable for both human and/ or animal studies. Ethical committees, Internal Review Boards, and guidelines followed must be named. When applicable, additional headings with statements on consent to participate and consent to publish are also required.
Not applicable.

Kalpana Sukumar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sureshkumar Selvaraj: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Moganesh Govindhan: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Director, BRICT Research Centre, B. Agraharam, Dharmapuri, Tamil Nadu, India.
